questions.question: Explain the concept of bus arbitration. Describe two methods of bus arbitration and compare their advantages and disadvantages.
keyPoints: Bus arbitration manages access to the bus to prevent conflicts
Daisy-chain arbitration uses priority encoding but is less scalable
Centralized arbitration has a dedicated arbiter but can become a bottleneck
Distributed arbitration (e.g., collision detection) avoids central failure but may have collisions

questions.question: Differentiate between hardwired and microprogrammed control units. Which one is preferred in modern processors and why?
keyPoints: Hardwired control uses combinational logic for fast execution but is inflexible
Microprogrammed control uses firmware for easier modification and debugging
Modern processors often use a hybrid approach for flexibility and performance
Microprogrammed control allows complex instruction sets and easier design changes

questions.question: Draw and explain the timing diagram for a typical memory read operation on a system bus. Discuss the role of control signals such as RD, WR, and ACK. How do wait states affect the timing?
keyPoints: Timing diagram shows address valid, data transfer, and control signal phases
RD signal indicates read operation, WR indicates write operation
ACK or READY signal acknowledges data transfer and插入 wait states
Wait states (via READY) extend the bus cycle to accommodate slower devices

questions.question: Discuss the organization of the system bus in a computer. Explain the functions of data bus, address bus, and control bus. Also, compare PCI, ISA, and SCSI buses in terms of data width, speed, and typical applications.
keyPoints: Data bus transfers data, address bus carries memory addresses, control bus carries timing and control signals
PCI (Peripheral Component Interconnect) offers high speed (up to 533 MB/s) and 32/64-bit width, used for modern peripherals
ISA (Industry Standard Architecture) is slower (8.33 MB/s) and 16-bit, legacy for older devices
SCSI (Small Computer System Interface) supports high-speed data transfer (up to 640 MB/s) for storage devices
